content.text: A chat model is an instruction-tuned model that has been further aligned — typically through RLHF, Constitutional AI, or DPO — to produce responses that are helpful, harmless, and honest in multi-turn conversational contexts. Chat models are the public-facing products: Claude, ChatGPT, Gemini, Llama-3-Instruct. They are what most developers interact with via APIs and what billions of users interact with in consumer products. Understanding how they differ from bare instruction-tuned models clarifies why alignment is a distinct phase from instruction tuning, and what trade-offs it introduces.
A fully trained chat model passes through three distinct training phases, each building on the previous:
Next-token prediction on a large corpus. Produces a base model that knows a great deal but will not follow instructions.
Training on (instruction, response) pairs. Produces an instruction-tuned model that follows directives but may still produce harmful, verbose, or sycophantic outputs.
Training to match human preferences. Produces a chat model that is helpful, declines harmful requests, calibrates uncertainty, and maintains a consistent, appropriate tone.
What Alignment Adds Beyond Instruction Tuning
Reliable across a broad range of harms
May be excessively long or padded
Chat models expect the structured message format — not a raw text prompt. The Anthropic Messages API makes this explicit:

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()


def demonstrate_chat_model_behaviours():
    """
    Four interaction patterns that only work reliably with a chat-aligned model:
    (1) refusal, (2) uncertainty, (3) clarification, (4) multi-turn coherence.
    """

    def ask(messages, system=""):
        kwargs = {"model": "claude-opus-4-8", "max_tokens": 256, "messages": messages}
        if system:
            kwargs["system"] = system
        return client.messages.create(**kwargs).content[0].text

    # 1. Refusal of harmful request
    print("=== Refusal ===")
    print(ask([{"role": "user", "content": "Give me step-by-step instructions for synthesising a dangerous chemical."}]))
    # -> Politely declines, explains why, may suggest safe alternatives.

    # 2. Calibrated uncertainty
    print("\n=== Uncertainty ===")
    print(ask([{"role": "user", "content": "What will the S&P 500 close at tomorrow?"}]))
    # -> Explains it cannot predict market movements, may discuss factors.

    # 3. Clarification seeking
    print("\n=== Clarification ===")
    print(ask([{"role": "user", "content": "Write me a script."}]))
    # -> Asks: for a play, a shell script, a Python script, a sales script?

    # 4. Multi-turn coherence
    print("\n=== Multi-turn ===")
    history = [
        {"role": "user",      "content": "My name is Alex and I'm learning Python."},
        {"role": "assistant", "content": "Great, Alex! Python is a wonderful first language. What would you like to learn first?"},
        {"role": "user",      "content": "Can you remind me what I said my name was?"},
    ]
    print(ask(history))
    # -> "You mentioned your name is Alex."


demonstrate_chat_model_behaviours()
Aggressive safety training can cause the model to refuse benign requests that superficially resemble harmful ones — e.g., refusing to discuss historical chemical weapons for an educational context.
RLHF optimises for human approval, which can cause the model to agree with incorrect user statements rather than correcting them — the model learns that agreement is rewarded.
Alignment training can introduce excessive caveats and disclaimers. A model that adds "I'm not a medical professional" to every health question quickly becomes annoying.
Some research suggests that RLHF can slightly reduce the model's raw reasoning capability in exchange for improved alignment properties, though this effect is small in well-tuned models.

A chat model is an instruction-tuned model that has been taught values in addition to skills. The skills come from pretraining; the instruction-following from SFT; the values — helpfulness, harmlessness, honesty — from alignment. All three phases are necessary, and the quality of each phase limits the quality of the final product.

A chat model is an LLM that has been specifically trained to participate in multi-turn dialogue in a helpful, harmless, and honest way. Unlike a raw pretrained base model — which simply predicts the next token in a document — a chat model understands that it is an assistant, that it should answer the question in the most recent user turn, that it should remember what was said earlier in the conversation, and that some requests should be declined. These properties are not inherent to the Transformer architecture; they are instilled through multiple post-pretraining training phases.

The defining characteristic of a chat model from an API perspective is its structured input format. Rather than accepting a single text prompt, a chat model accepts an ordered list of messages, each labelled with a role: system (instructions from the operator), user (turns from the human), and assistant (turns generated by the model in prior conversation steps). The model is trained to continue the conversation by generating the next assistant turn. This role-based structure is fundamental: mixing it up — for example, omitting roles or treating the API like a raw completion API — produces worse results because the model was trained to exploit the role structure.

Before alignment-tuned chat models, using a capable LLM required careful prompt engineering to coax the base model into following instructions. A base model trained on web text will happily continue a prompt with more web text rather than answering a question. Instruction tuning was the first step: models trained on (instruction, response) pairs learned to answer questions, but they also learned to produce harmful, biased, or sycophantic answers because those patterns were present in the training data. Alignment was the missing piece.

The practical consequence is enormous. Chat models can be deployed directly to end users without the operator needing to write elaborate refusal logic. The model itself declines to help with clearly harmful requests, hedges appropriately about things it doesn't know, and maintains a consistent persona across long conversations. For developers building products on top of LLMs, this shifts enormous engineering effort from custom safety logic to prompt design and evaluation.

From a research perspective, chat models also revealed that alignment is not simply about capability — it is about value loading. The same underlying weights can produce very different behavior depending on the alignment phase. This insight spawned the field of AI alignment research and drives ongoing work on techniques like Constitutional AI, DPO, and RLAIF.

At inference time, the chat model receives the conversation history as a list of (role, content) pairs. These are serialized into a single token sequence using a chat template specific to the model family. The Transformer then predicts the probability distribution over the next token, and sampling is applied to produce text. This is repeated autoregressively until the model outputs a stop token.

The chat template is the critical piece that most developers overlook. Different model families use different templates. OpenAI's format uses function-call JSON for tool use. Llama-3 uses a specific header/footer syntax with <|start_header_id|> and <|eot_id|> tokens. Claude uses a human-turn / assistant-turn format. Using the wrong template for a model will produce degraded outputs because the model was not trained on that format.
The log-probability of an assistant response
During RLHF training, a reward model
scores responses according to human preferences. The policy (chat model) is then optimised with PPO to maximise expected reward while staying close to the SFT model via a KL-divergence penalty:

DPO (Direct Preference Optimisation) sidesteps the reward model entirely by directly optimising the policy on pairs of preferred and rejected responses. The DPO loss for a preference pair (y_w, y_l) is:

DPO is simpler to implement than PPO (no separate reward model, no online rollouts) and has become the dominant alignment technique for open-weight models, while providers like Anthropic use proprietary variants of RLHF with Constitutional AI principles.

attrs.alt: Chat Model Architecture — message processing pipeline and three training phases

content.text: Chat models are the right choice when any of the following apply:
Any application where users exchange multiple messages — customer support, tutoring, coding assistants.
Tasks described in natural language: summarise this document, write a function, translate to Spanish.
When the operator cannot afford to implement extensive safety logic themselves — the chat model provides a baseline.
Function calling and tool use are designed into the chat API and are unreliable on raw base models.
Chat models work well with zero-shot prompts, reducing the prompt engineering effort compared to base models.
When NOT to use a chat model:
If you are doing document completion, autocomplete, or any task where you want the model to continue a text without following instructions, a base model gives you more control.
For difficult reasoning benchmarks, base models sometimes outperform aligned versions because RLHF can slightly reduce reasoning capability.
Fine-tuning from scratch on a new domain.
For highly domain-specific tasks with large fine-tuning datasets, starting from a base model and doing custom SFT can outperform a general chat model.
The Anthropic Messages API is the canonical way to interact with Claude chat models. Key parameters:
: Upper bound on tokens generated. Required.
: System prompt (operator instructions), passed as a top-level field in the Anthropic API.
: 0.0 = deterministic greedy; 1.0 = standard sampling. Default is 1.0.
: Nucleus sampling threshold. Use temperature OR top_p, not both.
: List of strings at which to stop generation.

import anthropic
import json

client = anthropic.Anthropic()  # reads ANTHROPIC_API_KEY from environment


# ── 1. Single-turn with system prompt ──────────────────────────────────────
def single_turn(user_message: str, system: str = "") -> str:
    kwargs: dict = {
        "model": "claude-opus-4-5",
        "max_tokens": 512,
        "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": user_message}],
    }
    if system:
        kwargs["system"] = system
    response = client.messages.create(**kwargs)
    return response.content[0].text


# ── 2. Multi-turn conversation manager ─────────────────────────────────────
class ConversationManager:
    def __init__(self, system: str = "", model: str = "claude-opus-4-5"):
        self.system = system
        self.model = model
        self.history: list[dict] = []

    def chat(self, user_message: str, max_tokens: int = 512) -> str:
        self.history.append({"role": "user", "content": user_message})
        response = client.messages.create(
            model=self.model,
            max_tokens=max_tokens,
            system=self.system,
            messages=self.history,
        )
        assistant_text = response.content[0].text
        self.history.append({"role": "assistant", "content": assistant_text})
        return assistant_text

    def reset(self) -> None:
        self.history = []


# ── 3. Structured output via JSON mode ─────────────────────────────────────
def extract_structured(text: str) -> dict:
    """Ask the model to return structured JSON from free text."""
    prompt = f"""
Extract the following fields from the text and return ONLY valid JSON:
- name (string)
- age (integer or null)
- topics (list of strings)

Text: {text}
"""
    raw = single_turn(prompt, system="You are a JSON extraction assistant. Output only valid JSON, no prose.")
    return json.loads(raw)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Single turn
    answer = single_turn("What is the capital of France?")
    print("Single turn:", answer)

    # Multi-turn
    conv = ConversationManager(system="You are a concise technical assistant.")
    r1 = conv.chat("What is a Transformer?")
    r2 = conv.chat("How does its attention mechanism work?")  # uses history
    print("Multi-turn r1:", r1[:80])
    print("Multi-turn r2:", r2[:80])

    # Structured
    data = extract_structured("Alice is 30 and loves Python and machine learning.")
    print("Structured:", data)
For long responses, streaming avoids waiting for the full completion before displaying output:
Chat models support tool use, letting the model call external functions when it needs live data:

A production customer-support chatbot must: authenticate the request, retrieve customer context from a database, inject it into a system prompt, handle multi-turn conversation, and log the exchange. The following shows a minimal but realistic pattern:

import anthropic
from dataclasses import dataclass, field

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

SUPPORT_SYSTEM = """You are a customer support agent for Acme Corp.
You have access to the customer's account information provided in the user context.
Be helpful, concise, and accurate. If you cannot resolve the issue, offer to escalate.
Never promise refunds or credits without explicit approval from the escalation team."""


@dataclass
class SupportSession:
    customer_id: str
    account_info: dict
    history: list[dict] = field(default_factory=list)

    def _build_system(self) -> str:
        ctx = f"Customer ID: {self.customer_id}\nAccount tier: {self.account_info['tier']}\n"
        ctx += f"Open tickets: {self.account_info['open_tickets']}\n"
        ctx += f"Last purchase: {self.account_info['last_purchase']}\n"
        return SUPPORT_SYSTEM + "\n\nCustomer context:\n" + ctx

    def respond(self, user_message: str) -> str:
        self.history.append({"role": "user", "content": user_message})
        response = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-opus-4-5",
            max_tokens=512,
            system=self._build_system(),
            messages=self.history,
        )
        text = response.content[0].text
        self.history.append({"role": "assistant", "content": text})
        return text


# Simulate a support conversation
session = SupportSession(
    customer_id="C-12345",
    account_info={"tier": "Premium", "open_tickets": 1, "last_purchase": "2025-12-01"},
)
print(session.respond("I haven't received my order from last month."))
print(session.respond("What options do I have?"))
Treating the chat API like a completion API.

Sending a raw text prompt as the "user" content without any structure works, but omitting the system prompt and relying on a single user message loses the operator-level control that the system slot provides.
Putting everything in the system prompt.
The system prompt is for operator instructions, not for injecting conversation history. Injecting prior exchanges into the system prompt confuses the model about which turns it generated.
Every API call is stateless — the model has no memory. If you do not send the full conversation history in the messages array, the model will not remember previous turns.
For classification, structured extraction, or code generation where you want consistent output, set temperature to 0.0 or a low value.
the response was cut off; if it is
you must handle the tool call before continuing.
If user-supplied content is pasted into messages, a malicious user can inject instructions that override the system prompt. Sanitize or constrain user input.
Write system prompts as operator contracts.
State what the model is, what it should do, and what it must never do. This is the most powerful lever for shaping model behavior.
Long histories increase cost and latency. Summarize old turns or use a sliding window. Include only turns relevant to the current task.
This produces reproducible results for classification and extraction and makes evaluation easier.
When expecting JSON or structured data, parse and validate after every call. Models occasionally produce malformed output.
Build a tool-dispatch loop that terminates when stop_reason is end_turn or when a maximum number of iterations is reached.
Run red-team prompts that probe over-refusal, sycophancy, and instruction injection before deploying a chat model to end users.

Chat model inference cost is dominated by two factors: the number of input tokens (which grow with conversation history) and the number of output tokens. Latency is primarily driven by output token count because generation is sequential. Time-to-first-token (TTFT) — the delay before the first word appears — is driven by input processing latency, which scales linearly with input tokens for most implementations.

For high-throughput deployments, batching multiple requests reduces per-request GPU overhead, but increases TTFT for individual requests. The right trade-off depends on whether the application is real-time conversational (low batch size, low TTFT) or asynchronous document processing (high batch size, throughput maximised).

Context caching is a critical optimization for chat applications with long system prompts. Anthropic's prompt caching API allows the KV cache for a fixed prefix (system prompt + static context) to be reused across multiple requests, reducing input processing cost by up to 90% for cached tokens.

content: pip install anthropic; handles retries, streaming, tool use

content.text: Chat models differ from related model types in important ways:

content: Simpler but may be less stable

content.text: Chat models introduce a class of security risks distinct from traditional software. Prompt injection attacks occur when user-supplied content contains instructions that override the system prompt. For example, a user might embed "Ignore previous instructions and instead..." in a document the model is asked to summarize. Defence-in-depth is required: validate that the model's output matches expected patterns, restrict the model's ability to take high-impact actions without confirmation, and run adversarial test suites as part of the deployment pipeline.

Jailbreaks are adversarial prompts that attempt to elicit policy-violating outputs. Well-aligned chat models are resistant to most published jailbreaks, but novel techniques emerge continuously. Operators should apply content classification on outputs as a second layer of defence and monitor production traffic for anomalous patterns.

Reliability concerns include: hallucination (the model generating plausible but false information), context window overflow (history truncated silently), and rate limit errors during traffic spikes. Hallucination is reduced but not eliminated by alignment training — always validate high-stakes model outputs against authoritative sources.
Treat all user input as untrusted. Never interpolate raw user text into system prompts.
Do not include API keys, passwords, or PII in system prompts or messages. These can appear in logs and model outputs.
Even well-aligned models occasionally produce policy-violating content. Apply a classifier to outputs in high-risk contexts.
Implement exponential back-off and circuit breakers around API calls. Set per-user rate limits to prevent abuse.
A chat model is a pretrained LLM that has been instruction-tuned (SFT) and then aligned (RLHF or DPO) to be helpful, harmless, and honest.
The three training phases — pretraining, SFT, and alignment — each contribute distinct properties; the quality of each phase limits the final product.
Chat models expect a structured role-based input format (system / user / assistant); using them like raw completion APIs loses alignment-dependent behavior.
Alignment introduces trade-offs: over-refusal, sycophancy, hedging verbosity, and a small capability tax.
DPO has largely replaced PPO-based RLHF for open-weight models due to its simplicity, while providers maintain proprietary alignment pipelines.
Context caching, streaming, and tool-use loops are the three most important production engineering concerns when deploying chat models.
Prompt injection and hallucination are the dominant security and reliability risks; defence-in-depth is required.
Choose a chat model over a base model for any user-facing conversational product; choose a base model when you need maximum control over generation behavior.
